journal for which the name had originally been chosen. And he gave Dada (and Arp's abode) the façade of a corporate structure, using envelopes printed with the return address: Administration/Mouvement Dada/Zurich/Zeltweg 83.99

As part of this effort to forge a broader identity, the group staged the “first public Dada evening,"roo as Ball wrote, underscoring the word public, on 14 July 1916 at Zurich's Waag Hall, an old guildhouse on one of the city's main squares [fig. 1.12]. Hennings read poems, Arp offered a discussion of his paper collages; Ball, Huelsenbeck, Janco, and Tzara performed their simultaneous poem; and members of the group in various combinations recited chants nègres and performed costumed and masked dances [fig. 1.13]. The event also marked a movement away from the margins of culture, seen in the shift within the topography of the city from the seedy Niederdorf, where no respectable citizen would go, to a central area where the bourgeoisie was part of the potential audience. ¹or Yet if it was a step toward a more mainstream identity, it also made the dadaist assault on bourgeois pieties all the more direct.

Along with the other offerings of the evening, the core members read manifestos, appropriating a traditional form of public and political communication aimed at establishing principles.1o² But Ball's text turned on the group itself, mocking its ambitions and suggesting a kind of “dada hubris."1o3 "Just a word," declaimed Ball, "and the word a movement."

Very easy to understand....To make of it an artistic tendency must mean that one is anticipating complications. Dada psychology dada Germany cum indigestion and fog paroxysm, dada literature, dada bourgeoisie, and yourselves, honored poets, who are always writing with words but never writing the word itself, who are always writing around the actual point. Dada world war without end, dada revolution without beginning, dada, you friends and also-poets, esteemed sirs, manufacturers, and evangelists. Dada Tzara, dada Huelsenbeck, dada m'dada, dada m'dada dada mhm, dada dera dada, dada Hue, dada Tza.1º4

A first public event, a first manifesto, but also an ending, for despite its obscurity, the manifesto was intended and received, Ball wrote, as a "thinly disguised break with friends."1o5 Ball soon distanced himself from the group, leaving Zurich for the Ticino in the first of several leave-takings and bringing to a close the period of his greatest intellectual influence.1º6

With Ball's absence and the closing of the cabaret, Tzara emerged as a new leader, converting Ball's persona as cabaret master of ceremonies into a role as a savvy media

spokesman with grand ambitions. Tzara was "the romantic internationalist," wrote Huelsenbeck in his 192o history of Dada, "whose propagandistic zeal we have to thank for the enormous growth of Dada."1o7 As a result, it was largely through the filter of Tzara's efforts and writing that ideas about Dada were communicated to an audience outside of Zurich. While Ball had not articulated a specific theory or program for Dada, Tzara's writings marked a certain shift, distilling concepts that had emerged at the Cabaret into discernible principles capable of being communicated. Departing from the more mystical aspects of Ball's thought with its religious and alchemical imagery, Tzara, as discussed in the introduction to this volume, offered a declaration of resistance to various forms of social government. In his widely distributed “Manifeste Dada 1918," he wrote, “To impose your ABC is a natural thing, hence deplorable."ro8

## THE GALERIE DADA

In January 1917, in Ball's absence, the group organized the Première Exposition Dada (First Dada Exhibition) at the Galerie Corray [12]. Works by Arp, Janco, Hans Richter, and Adya and Otto van Rees were shown along with tribal pieces, and Tzara offered three lectures on modern art. Returning to Zurich for a few months,109 Ball surely visited the show for he soon joined the other members of the group in founding the Galerie Dada Hans Corray relinquished the rooms above the Sprüngli confectionery and chocolate emporium, which he used as his gallery space, and the first of three exhibitions, mixing avantgarde work by the members of the expressionist Sturm group and by the dadaists themselves with primitive and children's art, was opened in March.1º The new location represented a significant step away from the sordid Niederdorf district, and there were other signs as well of Dada's newly genteel aspect: programs were printed in advance, guest lists made, invitations distributed, and relatively high entry fees charged. A series of ambitious experimental soirées were planned, but on the weekends only, offering a respite from the relentless nightly performances of the Cabaret Voltaire; midweek, visitors were offered tours and, starting in May, afternoon tea. An evening café on the premises was called the Kandinsky Room. Reflecting on this period of Zurich activities from the stridently politicized atmosphere of Berlin of 192o, Huelsenbeck wrote: “As I think back on it now, an art for art's sake mood lay over the Galérie Dada—it was a manicure salon of the fine arts, characterized by tea-drinking old ladies trying to revive their vanishing sexual powers with the help of something mad.""i12 At the time, however, Ball described the Galerie as a “continuation of last year's idea of the cabaret," but with its “barbarisms” overcome."3 He seemed particularly pleased at the integration of “pictures, music, dances, poems” achieved."4 But in addition, the founding of the gallery clearly signaled a new centrality for dance and the visual arts within the group's activities.

## DANCE

At the opening festivities on 29 March 1917, Sophie Taeuber danced in a costume by Arp to Ball's sound poems [see p. 16] and Claire Walter performed a group of “expressionist" dances. The young women's teacher, Rudolf von Laban, one of the pioneers of the modern dance movement, sat in the audience with a fellow student, Mary Wigman. Laban's presence in Zurich, and the network of personal and creative relationships that developed between the members of the Voltaire group and the Laban dancers, fostered a new attention to experimental dance within the Dada fold. Laban's keen interest in primitive ritual and non-narrative forms created grounds for an alliance. Dance found a place within the larger mandate for abstraction—offering the means, prompted by what was seen as the modern debasement of language, to initiate a shift from verbal communication to corporeal expression

Laban suggested that dance provided access to subrational layers of consciousness. Brought up in Bosnia, Laban spoke of his interest in the dances of dervishes seen in his youth“who perform their prayers not in words, but in body movements and especially endless turnings... at first sight, [they are] completely incomprehensible, even repulsive in the wild whirling which goes on till the dancers froth at the mouth. It all seems quite mad to us, but it is probably in the madness that the sense lies."ös Dances at the Galerie Dada simulated this ecstatic state. In a note on Taeuber's performance in the first issue of Dada, Tzara stressed the transgression of norms, describing Taeuber's dance as “delirious strangeness in the spider of the hand vibrating quickly ascending towards a paroxysm of a mockingly capriciously beautiful madness."ı6 To be sure, one finds many paroxysms of various kinds in Zurich Dada—but it is precisely conjuring a state of consciousness outside of quotidian awareness of the world that seemed, as Laban thought, to offer an antidote to the “warped psyche of our time."117

Not surprisingly, Ball was enthusiastic in his response, writing effusively after Taeuber's opening performance:

Abstract dances: a gong beat is enough to stimulate the dancer's body to make the most fantastic movements....The nervous system exhausts all the vibrations of the sound, and perhaps all the

hidden emotion of the gong beater too, and turns them into an image. Here, in this special case, a poetic sequence of sounds was enough to make each of the individual word particles produce the strangest visible effect on the hundred-jointed body of the dancer.18

For Ball, dance offered an important complement to his own experiments with sound poetry. Rhythm, whether produced by percussive instrument or phonic line of sound poetry, served as a form of direct corporeal stimulation—an almost electrical circuit through the nervous system——which would avoid intellectual mediation and forge instead a nonlinguistic emotional conduit of expression.

## TAEUBER AND ARP

Among the most important contributions in the visual arts were the far-reaching innovations in abstraction developed in the work of Hans Arp and his companion Taeuber. The two met in 1915 during Arp's first exhibition in Zurich at the Galerie Tanner, where he showed with Otto and Adya van Rees, and were soon inseparable. Taeuber, the only Swiss citizen within the Zurich Dada group, was a teacher at the Kunstgewerbeschule (School of Applied Arts). For Arp, the encounter initiated a shift from the type of work shown at the Galerie Tanner, which extended the faceted geometries of cubism to new media and a greater abstraction [2, 4, 5], toward more unprecedented forms of work, which often reflected Taeuber's training and influence. (Arp wrote that in 1915, Taeuber “had already freed herself from traditional art."119) Between 1916 and 1918, the couple developed a broad creative partnership, collaborating on paintings, collages, graphic work, and embroideries. Arp described their common goal as that of breaking with aesthetics” to produce work that would have more of the status of ordinary objects.

As part of this pursuit, Taeuber and Arp challenged the hierarchy between “applied” and “fine” art, often mobilizing the techniques and media of decorative arts in a deliberate shift toward a “minor" key.° For her part, Taeuber continued to devote equal time to both, teaching at the design school as well as working alongside Arp; and she made pillowcases and purses as well as paintings. Both she and Arp deliberately obscured the distinction, creating embroideries that were framed and hung on the walls like paintings [17-20].1² Arp defined their use of a medium with both decorative and feminine associations as a critical strategy, a deliberate resistance to aesthetic hubris: “Sophie Taeuber and I decided to completely renounce the use of

oil colors in our compositions. We wanted to avoid any reminiscence of canvas painting, which we regarded as characteristic of a pretentious and conceited world."2 What Arp described was a kind of programmatic modesty, and this rhetoric of humility permeates his writing.

Taeuber offered a similar challenge to expectations in a highly idiosyncratic group of sculptural works, dating from 1916 to 1918: Chalice, Amphora, Poudrier, and Dada Bowl [27-30]. Made of turned wood, a decorative arts medium, and evoking functional objects through their titles, they nonetheless stood as independent sculpture in a quiet transgression of boundaries.23 Yet if abstraction in painting was still new, the degree of abstraction seen in these sculptural objects was almost unprecedented.124 And extremely rare as well was the use of strict symmetry, here determined by the mode of fabrication. Symmetry is an idea foreign to traditional composition in the fine arts, which depends on the idea of a balance of component elements orchestrated through the subjective judgment of the artist: sculptural contrapposto—the asymmetrical positioning of the figure to suggest movement—stands as a case in point. (Arp, too, experimented with symmetry in a body of woodcuts, which appeared in Huelsenbeck's Phantastische Gebete [Fantastic Prayers] [see p. 426] and in Dada 1.) On both counts— three-dimensional abstraction and symmetry—the decorative or applied provided a radical logic for the fine arts.

A long tradition of abstraction existed within the category of the decorative, but it was precisely this transposition of strategies from one sphere to another that had far-reaching implications. Nowhere is this more in evidence than in the couple's preoccupation with the form of the grid during the Dada years from 1916 to 1918. Arp makes clear that the impetus for this work came from Taeuber herself, writing: "In 1915, Sophie Taeuber's extreme simplifications. her exclusive use of rectangular planes placed horizontally and vertically in the pictures she made at the time, exercised a decisive influence on my work."125 Taeuber's own interest in the grid as a strategy likely came from her arts and crafts training, where the use of all-over patterning was common and the warp and weft of textiles defined a grid structure But as an idea about a picture, the same gesture was something very new.

In 1918, the pair produced in collaboration a group of "duo-collages" [24], which stand among the first rigorous grids in the history of twentieth-century art, preceding by several months even Piet Mondrian's experiment with the form.26 Arp's description of the process of making the duo-collages stresses the artists' pursuit of a mechanical impersonality. Seeking to create an “anonymous and collective art," the pair jettisoned the use of scissors for the way in which “they all too readily betrayed the life of the hand," and used a paper cutter instead.127 The grid itself further minimized the subjective choice of the artist, creating instead a model of the artist as a restricted actor within a variable system—a counterweight to the valorization of judgment of which the dadaists were so skeptical.

Yet if the grid's structure offered one form of limit on an artist's judgment, chance provided another. Beginning in 1916 or 1917, Arp introduced fortuitousness into the creation of a group of collages [21–23]. Both Huelsenbeck and Richter state that Arp made these works by tearing paper into pieces, which he dropped to the floor and pasted into place where they fell,128 though the delicate balance seen in the finished works suggests that Arp exerted much greater control.129 Nonetheless, these works introduce visual signs of disorder into the orderly system of the grid in the suggestions of random dispersal, the soft jagged edges of torn paper [21], and wobbling structures [22]. Arp sets these noncompositional strategies against each other, using chance as a way of introducing irregularity into the systematic structure of the grid, without fully conceding to the artist's subjective discernment. For Arp, the random and accidental offered a way of letting go of conscious control and a point of access to the subrational. "The law of chance,"” he later wrote, “which comprises all other laws and surpasses our understanding...can be experienced only in a total surrender to the unconscious."13º

In 1917, there is a dramatic shift in Arp's work first seen in ten woodcuts he made for Tzara's Vingt-cinq Poèmes (Twenty-Five Poems) [8]. Moving away from the systematic structure of the grid, the free-form shapes in these images have a certain organic quality, suggesting indeterminate natural forms fixed in a moment of flux. The change seems to have occurred in Ascona, where Arp, Ball, and Janco all went frequently to visit Arthur Segal, Viking Eggeling, Laban, and other colleagues, and was inspired by Arp's encounter with the natural environment there: "In this same year [1917] I turned away from symmetrical woodcuts and embroideries....At Ascona I drew—with brush and ink— broken-off branches, roots, grasses, stones which the lake had thrown up on the shore. I simplified these forms and united their essences in moving ovals, symbols of the eternal metamorphosis and change of bodies."131 Two large ink drawings closely corresponding to this description are

Marss  e r  n M n n lon Wo    ich

![](images/76c92fdcc4bff232d5e5ce15dc611fb061ab70becb6791a2b2a86a510798422e.jpg)

included in this book [32, 33]. Yet what began as a form of abstraction from nature seems to have developed into a selfgenerative or “automatic” process, in which Arp moved his brush over the surface of the paper quickly, attempting to avoid conscious inhibition or control in the creation of form.

Arp's free-form drawings provided a foundation for what would become a key genre in his career—the abstract wooden relief [34-39, 50–52]. The reliefs were probably initially seen at the Kunstsalon Wolfsberg in September 1918 [fig. 1.14]. In these works, Arp distanced the making of objects not only from his conscious control, but also from his hand. A close friend of the artist related that Arp generated forms from automatic drawings. A carpenter then cut out the shapes with a band saw, and Arp assembled and painted them.3² Each was constructed of three or four layers of wood, and up to seven colors applied in commercial paints. The insistent roughness of these early reliefs—seen in the visible screws that held the works together, their rough sawed edges, and the flat, unmodulated application of paint in a commercial palette—all spoke to Arp's deliberate disregard for technical virtuosity, which would elevate the status of the thing made. Plant Hammer [39], in which amoebalike forms are attached to a painted board, was one of the first reliefs. The backing surface here maintains some of the function of the traditional frame, isolating

the space of artwork and alluding to the metaphor of a view through a window. Yet several of the curvilinear relief forms cross the boundary established by the backboard, suggesting a new permeability between the space of the artwork and the viewer's own. Arp soon dispensed with the backing plane, hanging the reliefs directly against the wall and creating a new hybrid form, which itself transgressed the boundaries between media. Neither painting nor sculpture, they seemed to exult in their status in between.

Such unboundedness is reiterated in the formal character of both the reliefs and automatic drawings—in the lack of ruled lines, and layers spilling over each other. Arp felt the organic provided a new model, which allowed him to replace the pretensions of logic with the productive nonlogic of natural growth, and absolutes with flux, writing: “Dada aimed to destroy the reasonable deceptions of man and recover the natural and unreasonable order. Dada wanted to replace the logical nonsense of the men of today by the illogically senseless.... Dada is for the senseless, which does not mean nonsense. Dada is senseless like nature. Dada is for nature and against art."33 But nature also helped him to articulate a new conception of form in his work that resisted gestalt.34 Warping, buckling, tending toward a loss of boundaries, it offered a counter idiom to the perniciousness of reason.

## THE MASK REDUX

Masks continued be an important source of inspiration in the period after the close of the Cabaret, and for several members of the group, they offered a specific challenge to traditional notions of portraiture based on physical resemblance. Between 1918 and 192o, following the creation of her marionettes, Taeuber made a group of five “dada heads" from turned and painted wood (four are extant [43–45, 47]). With heads sitting on round pedestals, they evoked the genre of the portrait bust——and several were later named "Portrait of Arp." Characteristically deploying the media of applied arts in a critical manner, Taeuber undercut any pretense of high seriousness by flirting with function—Arp is said to have joked that they could always be used as hat stands.35 And instead of resemblance, the heads offered playful abstraction, with facial designs reflecting the influence of oceanic or northwest Indian masks, in a witty refusal to locate individual identity in exterior physiognomy. (The possibility of an even more fully abstract rendering is proposed in a related painting in which the design for one head is transcribed as a projection on a flat plane [48].) A portrait Taeuber arranged to have taken by the studio photographer Nic[olas or Nikolai] Aluf in 1926 [46] suggests how the masklike Dada heads offered commentary on the contemporary. In the photograph, Taeuber's face, wearing a chic hat and veil, appears behind a wooden Dada head lettered with the inscription "Dada 192o." The juxtaposition sets up a critical counterpoint between the civilized façade of fashion rendered photographically and primitivizing abstraction—one a mask of external appearances, the other seen to be more revealing of internal truths.

Hans Richter, who joined the group in late 1916 after sustaining serious injuries on the front, linked the mask and access to the unconscious in a group of paintings shown at the Kunstsalon Wolfsberg [40, 41] in 1918, along with Arp's and Janco's reliefs. Called "visionary portraits," they were executed quickly—at a pace of three or four a day—and at twilight “"when the colors on [his] palette were almost indistinguishable." Richter claimed that the darkness forced a retreat from the visible world to an internal one and that the process of painting itself took on the character of “a sort of self-hypnosis."i36 Presented as depictions of Dada comrades, the results were gestural to the point of near unintelligibility, offering an assault on defined form. Yet, what was discernible resembled tribal masks and seemed to reveal the underlying primitive identity of the portrait subject.

## DADA INTERNATIONAL

Definitive changes occurred within the group between late 1917 and what might be seen as the demise of Zurich Dada around the end of 1919. The ending of the war allowed for the increased possibility of foreign travel, facilitating greater contact with the world outside of Zurich and offering the first real opening for an international expansion of Dada as a movement. The arrival in Zurich of two key figures, French-Spanish artist Francis Picabia and the German critic Walter Serner [fig. 1.15], was influential in these late stage developments, introducing a new cynically subversive tone.

Having spent much of the war in New York working closely with Marcel Duchamp and members of the Stieglitz circle, Picabia left for Europe in 1918, staying in Barcelona and Paris, all the while publishing his journal 391 [56, 283, 284], which had already become an international avantgarde forum. Both the journal and his own itinerancy made Picabia an important conduit for ideas. In 1g18 he sought treatment for neurasthenia (and perhaps for alcoholism) in Switzerland, first at Gstaad, then in the spa town of Bex-les-Bains, where he received a letter from Tzara inviting participation in Zurich Dada, copies of Dada 3 [13], and books of poetry as gifts.137 The exchange seems to have prompted Picabia and his wife Gabrielle Buffet to visit Zurich for three

Wa  t      at

![](images/d0624d7b4b5bda8b57494698627a2fce5d675dc22b83915ae285acdcb2da81ab.jpg)

weeks at the beginning of 1918,138 and the encounter between Picabia and the Zurich group was one of mutual recognition and embrace. The group descended upon Picabia en masse in his Zurich hotel room. To their delight, he smashed an alarm clock (the symbol par excellence of Swiss efficiency and rationality) and dipped its inner workings in ink, pressing them on paper to make monoprints (see Réveil matin I [Alarm Clock I]) [55]. Thus was European dadaism introduced to the imagery of dysfunctional machines developed in New York during the war, bringing together the Zurich group's critique of governing systems with New York's subversion of icons of industrial capitalism. One of the clock images was featured on the cover of Dada 4–5 [54], the last issue to be produced in Zurich. The inside pages offered another drawing by Picabia titled “Mouvement Dada"—a diagrammatic rendering of the avant-garde network linking Zurich, New York, and Paris in which the movement of ideas was presented as a circuit of machinic forces. Already experienced in publishing a journal Picabia collaborated with Tzara in editing the issue, and it featured a truly international roster of contributors including, along with Tzara and Picabia themselves, Jean Cocteau, Pierre Reverdy, Raoul Hausmann, Huelsenbeck, André Breton, Serner, Eggeling, and Richter. While in Zurich, Picabia also edited the eighth number of 391, which featured the activities of the Zurich Dada group [56].

In their enthusiasm, the dadaists arranged to have Picabia show his mechanomorphic paintings alongside Arp's and Janco's reliefs and Richter's visionary portraits at the Kunstsalon Wolfsberg to be held in September 1918. The radical nature of these works, while strikingly different from anything produced in Zurich, nonetheless confirmed the dadaists' sense of kinship with the itinerant artist: Picabia used technical drawing and diagrams to create machine images, which presented themselves, through their titles and the suggestion of mindless, repetitive motion, as analogies for modern anomie. The director of the gallery however, was less enthusiastic and returned the paintings to Picabia before the opening. Tzara, embarrassed at the limits to Zurich's avant-garde sensibility, wrote to Picabia seeking to assuage him: “The Director of the Salon is an abominable and pretentious insect whose impudence and lack of talent have been etched like vermin on his feeble intellectual capacity."139 This failed episode aside, Picabia's stay in Zurich forged a first point of contact between the Dada group there and the avant-garde in New York, Paris, and Barcelona.

Serner, who had considerable intellectual authority in German-speaking cultural circles, had fled Berlin for Zurich in 1915 after having abused his professional credentials— he was a doctor of law—to forge a medical certificate for expressionist Franz Jung after the latter had deserted the front. Mingling within the same circles of displaced artists and writers who populated Zurich's cafés, Serner had at first kept a studied distance from the group around the Cabaret Voltaire. In Sirius, the cultural and political journal he founded with Christian Schad while in Zurich, Serner launched a scathing critique of Ball's and Huelsenbeck's proto-dada Berlin performances of 1915, deriding them for “blasphemy against the intellect... and its words."4º By 1918, however, he had abandoned his defense of rationality Together with Arp and Tzara, Serner began collaborating on several poems under the group title “Société Anonyme pour l’exploitation du vocabulaire dadaiste" (The Exploitation of Dadaist Language Company) and produced a fake press release about a duel between Tzara and Arp—the latter marking the invention of a key dadaist genre, which implicitly signaled the group's growing interest in both exploiting and intervening in a new media culture. In dozens of skillfully placed fake newspaper articles, Serner pursued a media pranksterism that undermined the objectivity and veracity on which modern journalism purported to be based.

On 9 April 1919, Serner joined Tzara and Richter— all that remained of the earlier Zurich group—on the stage at the soirée held at Kaufleuten Hall [see fig. 1.11], the final large-scale Dada event in Zurich. According to Richter's account, the audience, eager to be shocked, found the opening events—a lecture by Eggeling on abstract art, dances by Suzanne Perrottet in a mask by Janco, poems written by Huelsenbeck and Kandinsky—somewhat disappointing in this regard and watched with polite amusement. There was some improvement with the performance by twenty people of a simultaneous poem by Tzara and the “tunes or anti-tunes” of Hans Heusser, but Serner finally provided the provocation sought. He came onto the stage dressed in a morning suit, carrying a headless tailoring dummy, to which he offered a bouquet of flowers. He brought a chair, sat astride it with his back facing the audience and began to read his manifesto "Letzte Lockerung" (Final Dissolution). Now truly shocked, the audience verged on rioting, pelting Serner, who calmly continued reading, with insults and trash.14 The published version of this text takes the combustible dadaist manifesto genre to a new extreme, characterized by a mixture of bitter irony, obscenity, and crude aggressiveness, with more than a hint of violence. Serner's lines transform earlier dadaism's ethical despair into a new note of overarching cynicism:

Everything is a confidence game (“You see EVERYTHING is rastaquouèresque, my dear friends"); everything equally ridiculous (“That everyone falls metaphorically on his ass. And sneezes"), and the pretensions of privilege must be debunked (“The pancakes, the Bible sayings, the girls' bosoms, the percentages, the Gauguins, the snot-rags, the stocking garters, the spirits, the lavatory seats, the waistcoats, the bugs—all the stuff that simultaneously they do think of and pore over—must be shoved, one after another, so sharply in front of the curve of their chins until finally their feeling of well-being is as great as the mere feeling of wooziness they had experienced until then.")142

Labeled “a breviary for a confidence man” in its first publication, the manifesto introduces a new modern type, restless in his search for stimulation, who gains notoriety for his public acts, but whose motives are inscrutable. A critical notice, which appeared shortly after its first reading, spewed: "Messrs. the dadaists, who were never more unnecessary than presently, should confine their manifestoes to their numerous magazines... and not burden the public with them." But this protector of the public's sensibility was none other than Serner himself.143

In accordance with Tzara's ambitions for a broadbased movement, Serner attempted to found a branch of Dada in the fiercely anti-German city of Geneva, organizing there the First Dadaist World Congress in late 1919 and the Grand Dada Ball in 192o [57]. Both efforts proved financially disastrous. In hindsight, the most productive legacy of Dada in Geneva may lie in the support Serner provided for the work of Christian Schad, who seemed to have understood Dada as a committed battle against dogma in artistic practice; the two may have had a romantic alliance.44 Schad had shown paintings and woodcuts of a mystical expressionist bent at the Zurich Kunsthalle in October 1915, but in Geneva he began producing work that offered a nuanced reading of Arp's recent efforts. Schad created abstract wood reliefs—the Alsatian artist's signature genre—which developed on the principle of creating an object neither rarified through materials nor trained artistic technique by incorporating components associated with machine technology heavy enamel commercial paints, mass-produced metal hardware, and bits of metallic paper [58-60]. Schad's strategy of laying these elements on the surface seems to have led to another development more wholly his own. Reclaiming the photogram, a technique known since the discovery of photography but hitherto unused for artistic purposes, Schad created what are among the first intentionally abstract photographs [61–65]. (Tzara, who took a group of these images to Paris, most likely showing them to Man Ray, later dubbed them Schadographs to distinguish them from Man Ray's photograms called Rayographs.)145 Schad covered the light-sensitive surfaces of photographic paper with tiny compositions of the most quotidian detritus of modern life— scraps of fabric, lace, and rubber; printed paper and even balls of dust—and left them to develop on his basement windowsill, before bathing them in fixative. Schad recalled: My penchant for everything lying around on the streets, in shop displays, in cafés, even in waste bins was at its height."146 The resulting images took the new genre of collage a step further, automatizing the technique to create a trace of direct contact—a collage, that is, without the glue. Schad delighted in the way that the process, which minimized the artist's subjective investment and which, in the unpredictability of the result, allowed for a certain amount of chance, offered a challenge to traditional composition, producing instead images of a “lawless simplicity."147 Serner commented with satisfaction on the “dawning of technology in art."148 On many of these images, Schad cut a jagged border “to free them," he said, "from the convention of the square."149 Resembling the asymmetrical forms of his reliefs, these borders draw attention to the close relation between the two types of work, which combine technological elements, found materials, and abstraction in the first important reading of Zurich innovations.

The Kaufleuten Hall soirée stood as Zurich Dada's last hurrah. A quiet distance had been growing between the writers and media spokesmen Serner and Tzara, on the one hand, and the artists Arp, Janco, and Richter on the other. Soon afterward efforts at reformulation were seen, which codified the group's dissolution. Arp, Janco, and Richter founded the short-lived Bund Radikaler Künstler (Alliance of Revolutionary Artists), which was committed to abstraction as an expression of socialist political ideals in solidarity with the recent rebellions in Munich and Budapest, and later the less overtly political exhibiting society Neues Leben (New Life). By the end of r92o, Huelsenbeck had left for Berlin, Tzara for Paris, and Richter to his parents' estate at Klein-Kölzig with the filmmaker Eggeling. Ball was in his village, and only Arp and Janco remained in Zurich.

I am particularly grateful for the insightful comments and research assistance provided by Matthew Witkovsky, Amanda Hockensmith and Mark Levitch in preparing this essay. Walburga Krupp, curator of the Stiftung Hans Arp und Sophie Taeuber-Arp e.V., Rolandseck, Germany, has been extraordinarily generous with her knowledge and time in answering questions.

42 F. W. Mott was among the first to use the term “"shell shock" to describe traumatic psychic disturbances incurred in battle in the absence of visible, somatic injuries. F.W. Mott, Proceedinas of the Roual Societu of Medicine 9 (1g16), i-xxiv. World War I was the first conflict in which disability pensions were widely granted for psychological disorders. 5 Richard Terdiman has coined the term “memory crisis” to define the urgency of concern about memory in the modern period, evidenced in the work of a broad range of intellectuals including Ferdinand Tönnies, Charles Baudelaire, Marcel Proust, Georg Simmel Walter Benjamin, and Sigmund Freud Richard Terdiman, Present Past: Modernity and the Memory Crisis (Ithaca, 1993).

59 Ball, Flight Out of Time, 68. Entry for 18 June 1916.

60 Ball writes, "Magic is the last refuge of individual self-assertion, and maybe of individualism in general" (entry for 9 January 1917) and “The final result of individualism is magic" (entry for 28 February 1917). Flight Out of Time, 96, 99.

61 Ball, Flight Out of Time, 68. Entry for   
18 June rg16.

62 In his essay "The Storyteller," for instance, Walter Benjamin laments that the storyteller "has already become something remote from us and something that is getting even more distant." He continues: “It is as if something that seemed inalienable to us, the securest among our possessions, was taken from us: the ability to exchange experiences." Walter Benjamin, "The Storyteller” (1936) in Illuminations, ed. Hannah Arendt, trans. Harry Zohn (New York: 1955, 1968), 83. Similarly, Siegfried Kracauer sees the onslaught of technical media leading to a kind of cultural amnesia, noting that the "flood of photos sweeps away the dams of memory." Siegfried Kracauer, "Photography" (1927), in The Mass Ornament: Weimar Essays, trans. and ed. Thomas Y. Levin (Cambridge, Mass., 1995), 58. Herman Hesse notes "if the individual has not himself changed... then at least the ideals and fictions, the wishes and dreams, and the mythologies and theories that rule our intellectual life have...Irreplaceable things have been lost and destroyed forever." Herman Hesse, "The Longing of Our Time for a Worldview" (1926), The Weimar Sourcebook, ed. Anton Kaes, Martin Jay, and Edward Dimendberg (Berkeley, 1994), 365.

63 Ball, Flight Out of Time, 56. Entry for 12 March 1916.

67 Arp feigned madness before a German consular official after reaching Switzerland. Tzara may have obtained deferment because of a nervous condition from which he was suffering at the time.

96 The idea of branding emerged in the nineteenth century as the industrial. ization of goods moved from local communities to centralized factories: in an effort to familiarize a wider customer base beyond the reach of word of mouth with their products, manufacturers adopted brand names and logos to reassure customers of the reputation and quality of their goods. With the precipitous development of modern marketing as a discipline in the years 191o–25, however, producers began to publicize and reinforce brand identifications on a far broader scale. Morgen Witzel, "Introduction," Marketing (Bristol, 2ooo), vii-xxxvii. For a detailed look at the development of marketing and consumer culture in the United States, see Susan Strasser, Satisfaction Guaranteed: The Making of the American Mass Market (New York, 1g89).

"'Dada ist gross Dada ist schön,'" in Bolliger, Dada in Zürich, 25–27.

134 Looking at Arp in the surrealist context, Briony Fer suggests Arp identified with nature less as an ideal and more

as “a matter of collapsing forms and symbols." Briony Fer, "Laws of Chance," in On Abstract Art (New Haven, 1997), 55–76.

135 Gabrielle Mahn, "Tête DADA (1920) de Sophie Taeuber, Un manifeste plastique," Les Cahiers du Musée national d'art moderne 88 (Summer 2004), 65; citing Carola Giedion-Welcker, Plastik des 20. Jahrhunderts (Stuttgart, 1955), 83.

136 Richter, Dada Kunst und Antikunst (Cologne, 1964), 56. Here translated from the German edition.

137 On the significance of this type of epistolary contact within dadaism, see Matthew S. Witkovsky, "Pen Pals" in Dickerman, The Dada Seminars, 269-293.

138 William A. Camfield, Francis Pícabia: His Art, Life and Times (Princeton, N.J., 1979), 113–118.

139 Tzara letter to Francis Picabia, 26 September 1918, in Michel Sanouillet, Dada à Paris, 2 vols. (Paris, 1993), 469.

140 Walter Serner, Sirius (March 1916), cited in Green, ed., Blago Bung, 25.

141 Richter, Dada: Art and Anti-Art, 77–8o.

142 Serner "Last Loosening Manifesto," in Green, ed., Blago Bung, 155, 159–60.

143 Review by M. Ch. [pseudonym for Serner] in Berliner Börsen-Courier, no. 182 (17 April 1919), in Walter Serner, Das Gesamte Werk, vol. 2: Das Hirngeschwür, ed. Thomas Milch (Munich, 1982), 19-20.

144 On the dadaist Schad, also see Jill Lloyd, "Christian Schad: Reality and Illusion." and Olaf Peters, “A voluntary international madman': Christian Schad and the Avant-Garde, 1915– 20," in Christian Schad and the Neue Sachlichkeit, ed. Jill Lloyd and Michael Peppiatt {exh. cat., Neue Galerie] (New York, 2003), 15–28, 29-37.

145 Floris Neusüss points out that the original edition of the catalogue of Alfred Barr's 1936 exhibition Fantastic Art, Dada and Surrealism includes the line "Schadograph' is a term invented by Tzara, 1936," suggesting that the term was coined on that occasion. Floris Neusüss, “Des Corpes des Rayons," in Photogrammes (Paris, 19g8), unpaginated; cited in Peters, Christian Schad, 32.

146 Fotogramme—die lichtreichen Schatten, ed. Floris M. Neusüss Jexh. cat., Fotoforum Kassel] (Kassel, 1983), 18, cited in Peters, Christian Schad, 30.

147 Schad, "Autobiographische Notizen," in Schadographien, Die Kraft des Lichts [exh. cat., Museum moderner Kunst](Passau, 1999), 14.

148 Schad, "Picture Captions," in Lloyd and Peppiatt, eds., Christian Schad, 22r.

149 Bettina Schad, "Die Entstehung der Schadographien—ein vierzigjähriger künstlerischer Prozess," in Schadographien, 68–72 and 71–72.

(War Cripples [45% Fit for Service!]) is partly visible at left. Bildarchiv Preussischer Kulturbesitz

# BRIGID DOHERTY

## LEFT

Johannes Baader's assemblage Das grosse Plasto-Dio-Dada-Drama (The Great Plasto-Dio-Dada-Drama) in the second room of the First International Dada Fair, Berlin, 1920. Giovanni Lista, Paris

Early in 1917, Richard Huelsenbeck, a twenty-four-year-old German medical student and poet, returned to Berlin from Zurich, where he had spent the preceding year in the company of Hans Arp, Hugo Ball, Emmy Hennings, Marcel Janco, Hans Richter, Sophie Taeuber, Tristan Tzara, and others who took part in the beginnings of Dada at the Cabaret Voltaire. Back in the German capital in the midst of the acute material deprivation and increasing social and political tension brought on by World War I, Huelsenbeck soon began to collaborate with the artists and writers George Grosz, John Heartfield, Wieland Herzfelde, and Franz Jung in the publication of Neue Jugend (New Youth), a periodical that anticipated the posters, flyers, and magazines of Berlin Dada in form as well as content [84, p. 431]. On 22 January 1918, Huelsenbeck delivered his “Erste Dada-Rede in Deutschland” (First Dada Speech in Germany) and recited from his book of poems, Phantastische Gebete (Fantastic Prayers) [p. 426] as part of an “author's evening” at art dealer I. B. Neumann's gallery on the fashionable Kurfürstendamm. Over the course of the next few weeks Grosz, Heartfield, Herzfelde, and Jung joined him in founding the “Club Dada," which also counted Johannes Baader, Raoul Hausmann, Hannah Höch, Walter Mehring, and Otto Schmalhausen among its members. In the “First Dada Speech," Huelsenbeck recounted the origins, aims, and effects of Dada in Zurich. He asserted Dada's thoroughgoing “newness” and its aesthetic as well as ethical opposition to cubism, futurism, and, above all, expressionism. And he announced, using bitter, cynical, and deliberately contradictory rhetoric that would emerge as characteristic of Berlin Dada's proclamations: "We were against the pacifists, because it was the war that had given us the possibility to exist in all our glory. We were for the war, and today Dada is still for the war. Things have to collide: the situation so far is nowhere nearly gruesome enough."

On 12 April 1918, the Berlin dadaists staged an evening of lectures, poetry readings, and performances at the Berlin Sezession, which at that time was also host to a retrospective exhibition honoring the sixtieth birthday of German painter Lovis Corinth (1858–1925), a founder of the Munich Sezession, which favored alternatives to academic art exhibitions, and twice president of its Berlin counterpart (1911, 1915–25). Among the 140 oil paintings in Corinth's 1918 Berlin Sezession exhibition was Im Schutze der Waffen (Beneath the Shield of Arms) [fig. 2.1], a bombastic, patriotic work that represented what the artist described as his effort during the war to cultivate in his painting “a terrifying earnestness" that would "regenerate" German art and set it apart from the

BELIN

LPoe n et efee Se znt ie ot sild ofytss.  , o s s  tes Lanp nerg

![](images/653099bff564e727f11198a048d0e40d9d244a05e17a05a8c96011b8ab6ce02e.jpg)

art of other nations, especially France and Russia.² Corinth's nationalist politics, as well as his faith in the capacity of art, and of oil painting in particular, at once to remain meaningfully bound to tradition and to contribute significantly to the development of contemporary society, could not be more different from the internationalist and at least seemingly anti-art stance of the Berlin dadaists. World War I and its social and political upheaval shook the foundations of Corinth's faith in painting at the same time as those events helped generate unprecedented commercial interest in his art, especially his depictions of the landscape surrounding the Walchensee in Upper Bavaria. But if the great painter Corinth came to wonder whether art could continue to contain and convey meaning in contemporary German society, the artists of Berlin's Club Dada responded to the circumstances that aroused Corinth's doubt by producing works intended not only to break with, but virtually to destroy artistic tradition as well as the commercial market in which works of art circulated as commodities, or what Corinth called “mere objects of value."3

## DADAIST MANIFESTO

Speaking to an overflowing audience in the rooms where Corinth's paintings hung, Huelsenbeck offered what he called the “first theoretical account of the Dada principle." Titled, according to the evening's printed program and invitation, “Dadaismus im Leben und in der Kunst" (Dadaism in Life and Art), Huelsenbeck's presentation, which an unsympathetic critic described as having lasted an hour and a half, included a reading of his “Dadaistisches Manifest" (Dadaist Manifesto), a three-page flyer and the first Club Dada publication, signed copies of which were available for purchase that evening at a price of five marks.4 Grosz recited from his own lyric poems and danced in a new kind of production he called “Sincopations," a semi-Americanized misspelling intended as an homage to jazz music. In the evening's final presentation, Hausmann added to all the “firsts” announced by the Berlin dadaists in 1918 the novelty of his “Das neue Material in der Malerei" (The New Material in Painting), a manifesto later published and exhibited under the title Synthetisches Cino der Malerei (Synthetic Cinema of Painting) [93] at the Erste Internationale Dada-Messe (First International Dada Fair) in June 192o.

Contemporary newspaper reviews and the reminiscences of participants describe the events of the April 1918 Dada evening as tumultuous, indeed nearly riotous, both onstage and off. “The threat of violence hung in the air," wrote a reviewer for the Berliner Börsen-Courier. “One envisioned Corinth's pictures torn to shreds with chair legs. But in the end it didn't come to that. As Raoul Hausmann shouted his programmatic plans for dadaist painting into the noise of the crowd, the manager of the Sezession gallery turned the lights out on him."5 The threat of dismemberment that the reviewer believed Dada and its outraged audience posed to Corinth's paintings reiterated a central theme of the text Huelsenbeck presented that evening. The “Dadaist Manifesto” opens with the double assertion that works of art are not so much expressive of as shaped by the age in which they are produced, and that artists are the “creatures,” rather than, say, the geniuses, of their epoch. “Art" in the “Dadaist Manifesto” takes on specifically human characteristics—it “lives” in particular historical periods, and possesses a “consciousness" with particular “contents." Moreover, the art Huelsenbeck praises has, as he puts it, visibly “let itself be thrown by the explosions of the last week." Questions of intentionality, contingency, corporeality, and historicity are thus crucial to what Huelsenbeck promulgates as the “highest art” of his day, an art “which is forever gathering up its limbs after yesterday's crash." “The best and most extraordinary artists," he explains, "will be those who every hour snatch the tatters of their bodies out of the frenzied cataract of life, holding fast to the intellect of their time, bleeding from hands and hearts." “Dadaism," Huelsenbeck asserts, is the first art movement that “no longer confronts life aesthetically." Instead, the tatters of the contemporary artist's body and consciousness have their counterpart in the “slogans of ethics, culture, and interiority” that Dada “tears to pieces” in the new forms of poetry produced in Zurich and through the introduction of “new materials in painting" that both Huelsenbeck's manifesto and Hausmann's lecture advocated as part of the internationalist expansion of Dada in Berlin.⁶

The “Dadaist Manifesto" alludes to World War I as "the explosions of last week” and “yesterday's crash," asserting, simultaneously and almost paradoxically, both the war's presence and its historicity, its belonging at once to the moments of the manifesto's production and presentation, and to a more or less immediate past. The manifesto calls up the overwhelming physical and psychological effects of the war. In so doing it means to insist on the necessary subjection of contemporary artists to the collisions that produced those traumatic effects, as well as the necessary recognition, on the part of artists and their audiences, that those collisions had a history, that they belonged to the present as well as to a past that could be mapped, even if that past had taken place only yesterday, or last week. In insisting on the proximity as well as the historicity of the collisions it invokes, the "Dadaist Manifesto” presents the experience of the war as metonym of the experience of modernity, also understood as historical category, a period in which the dadaists recognized themselves to be immersed, and in relation to which they nonetheless sought, in their art and writing, to assume a position of critical distance. As Huelsenbeck bodied them forth in the Berlin Sezession's decorated rooms, "yesterday's crash" and “the explosions of last week" represented the present of the manifesto as did the war that was still raging, and the metropolitan modernity of Berlin in April 1918.7

Echoing the themes while simultaneously amplifying and dismantling the urgent rhetoric of Huelsenbeck's manifesto, the text Hausmann presented at the Dada evening in April 1918 takes up the call for the introduction of “new materials in painting" and aims specifically to distinguish Berlin Dada's use of so-called “new materials” from its cubist and futurist precedents. Describing Dada as "perfectly kindhearted malice, alongside exact photography the only legitimate pictorial form of communication and balance in shared experience," Hausmann offered these emphatic ungrammatical conclusions to the crowd assembled at the Berlin Sezession:

In Dada you will recognize your real situation: miraculous constellations in real material, wire, glass, cardboard, fabric, corresponding organically to your own utterly brittle fragility, your bagginess. Only here, and for the first time, there are no repressions, no axious obstinacies, we are far from the symbolic, from totemism; electric piano, gas-attacks, manufactured relations, men howling in military hospitals, whom we with our wonderful contradictory organisms for the first time help along to some kind of just compensation, spinning central axle, reason to stand or fall. Through the exquisite punch of our deployment of material as the newest art of a progressive selfpresentation captured in motion with the relinquishment of the traditional protections of those standing around in the vicinity, as embodied atmosphere.8

Thus Dada art emerged, at least prospectively, as an aggressively paradoxical (kindheartedly malicious) counterpart to the exactness of photography, a new kind of art that would at once mimic cinema and instantiate the “real situation," that is to say the bodily disposition, of contemporary viewers, some of whom, Hausmann suggested, would discover in Dada not only a version of the circumstances and character of their own unhappy corporeality, but also a therapeutic intervention into their lives that would restore to art an authentic ethics that the dadaists believed modern art had elsewhere transformed into nothing but slogans. The last unwieldy, incomplete sentence of Hausmann's text attempts to establish the relations among the dadaists, as makers of art, the material out of which they make their art, the beholders of the art they make, and the site or situation of all those relations, taken together. The dadaists, Hausmann says, make art out of material not previously put to such use. In so doing they develop a “progressive” form of “selfpresentation," or what he also calls “miraculous constellations” of “real material” that “correspond organically” to the beholder's own fragile, brittle, baggy body. Finally, the “exquisite punch" of Dada art, its “progressive self-presentation," is “captured," as if cinematographically, “in motion with" (which in Hausmann's terms would seem to mean, at once, “at the same time as," “in the company of," and “as a consequence of") its audience's letting go of conventional defenses (defenses, say, against getting punched in the first place). Moreover the apparently simultaneous making and viewing of the dadaists'“progressive self-presentation" is captured specifically as “embodied atmosphere," or as something like the (again paradoxical) quasi-cinematic presentation of the making and viewing of Dada's new art as the

work of art itself. Thus Hausmann proposed a renovation of the work of art with the aim of making art respond to the needs, in particular the physical needs, of contemporary viewers, who are represented in the text by the fragile, brittle, baggy “you” whom Hausmann addresses directly, and by the “men howling in military hospitals” he also mentions. Despite the urgency of Hausmann's call for the use of new materials in the production of works of art and in contradiction to his suggestion that the Berlin dadaists were already using such new materials, the earliest documented appearance of such materials in Hausmann's own production dates to spring 192o, and Hannah Höch's Dada-Plastik, a small sculptural assemblage of metal, wire, feathers, and patterned paper, was not made until 1919.9 Instead, by the time Hausmann re-titled his manifesto “Synthetic Cinema of Painting" [see 93] sometime after March 1919, the predominant form of Dada montage involved primarily the use of materials, including photographic illustrations, appropriated from the print media.

## THE INVENTION OF PHOTOMONTAGE

Not long after he spoke of “The New Material in Painting” to the outraged audience at the Berlin Sezession, Hausmann joined Hannah Höch for a summer holiday on the Baltic Sea. That vacation provided the occasion for what Hausmann and Höch, who were involved in a turbulent romantic relationship between 1915 and 1922, later described as the invention of photomontage, the medium in which Berlin Dada's most significant contributions to modern art were made. Given the importance of photomontage to Berlin Dada, it is worth considering what the dadaists had to say about how they came to deploy that technique, which encompasses the production of pictures consisting in whole or in part of photographs cut-and-pasted or otherwise combined or manipulated in the process of their making or presentation. According to Hausmann, he “began in summer 1918 to make pictures out of colored papers, newspaper clippings, and posters," which is to say that he started, shortly after advocating the introduction of “new material in painting," to work in a medium whose materials were, by the sound of it, all but identical to those that had been used, beginning in 1912, in cubist collage. They were subsequently used in diverse ways in the collage practices of the Italian futurists and the Zurich dadaists —all precedents Hausmann acknowledges in recounting how he came to make photomontages.1º Sometime later that summer of 1918, he and Höch rented a room in a fisherman's cottage in the village of Heidebrink on the island of

Usedom. It was there, Hausmann would explain in a memoir originally published in French in 1958, that he “conceived the idea of photomontage."

In nearly every house there was to be found hanging on the wall a color lithograph depicting an infantryman in front of military barracks. In order to render this memento of the military service of a male member of the family more personal, a portrait photograph of the owner of the martial image had been glued in place of the head in the lithograph. It was like a thunderbolt: one could—I saw it instantaneously—make pictures, assembled entirely from cut-up photographs. Back in Berlin that September, I began to realize this new vision, and I made use of photographs from the press and the cinema."

Höch sketched a similar story in notes she made for a 1966 lecture on Dada, in which she describes the dadaists' practice of photomontage as a way of “working systematically with photographic material." In Höch's recollections, Dada photomontage:

began with our seeing an amusing oleograph on the wall of our guest room in a fisherman's cottage on the Baltic Sea. It depicted—fitted in among the pompous emblems of the Empire—five standing soldiers in five different uniforms—yet photographed only once—upon whom the head of the fisherman's son had five times been glued. This naively kitschy oleograph hung in many German rooms as a memento of the son's service as a soldier. It provided the occasion for Hausmann to expand upon the idea of working with photographs. Immediately after our return we began to do pictorial photomontage.²

Prints that had been turned into photomontages like those Höch and Hausmann describe were commonly found in German homes before and during World War I. Höch herself kept one such picture and labeled it “The Beginning of Photomontage” [fig. 2.2]. Neither Höch nor Hausmann ever specified what the photomontages they say they made in September 1918 looked like. In particular, they never made clear whether or not the visual form of the first photomontages bore any resemblance to the popular images that inspired them. A montage painting exhibited at the First International Dada Fair in Berlin in 192o perhaps alludes to the role that souvenir soldier portraits played in the Berlin dadaists' purported invention of photomontage. Bauernbild (Farmer Picture), also known as Industriebauern (Industrial Farmers) 126] by Georg Scholz, an artist later acclaimed in association with the so-called Neue Sachlichkeit or “New Objectivity" in mid-192os painting, incorporates a military souvenir print like the ones described by Höch and Hausmann Heartfield and Grosz were especially eager to secure this picture for the Dada Fair, an exhibition of nearly two hundred Dada works that they organized jointly with Hausmann. Heartfield and Grosz sent a postcard to Scholz in mid-June 192o, urging him to pack up his Farmer Picture and ship it to Berlin for the “DADA show, biggest show on earth," set to open on Friday the 25th. (Behind schedule, the Dada Fair eventually opened on the 3oth).13

On the rear wall of the sparse farmhouse parlor in Scholz' 192o montage painting, above a plaster bust of Kaiser Wilhelm II set out on a modest wooden cupboard, hangs a picture. Framed but not ornately, it shows a bright blue and red uniform topped off with a pasted-on black-and-white photographic face. The placement of the hand-colored photomontage portrait within the larger montage painting suggests that it might be a portrait of a family member, perhaps a firstborn son fallen in battle and therefore absent from the gathering depicted in the foreground. (As noted below, we have reason to think that the figures Scholz portrays are not just “industrial" but specifically post-World War I farmers.) It hardly needs saying that the young man shown in the

framed montage does not resemble his family as shown by Scholz; the young man's apparent normality, underscored by his uniform and upright pose, seems strange in the midst of his monstrous relatives, whom Scholz has depicted in grotesque, overblown ietail. The photograph's setting, clearly a portrait studio, is unlike the background barracks described by Höch and Hausmann, and it is also unlike the peasant family's home. The young man stands in a simulacrum of a bourgeois interior. A carved and cushioned chair in the print offsets the farmhouse's simple wooden seats, and the young man's fingers (holding white gloves) rest with casual, pseudo-aristocratic elegance on a writing table, while his brother's, by contrast, trap a toad with knucklebending effort among other items that are set out before the family: a pasted-on conservative Christian newspaper, a holepunch, and a mass-produced porcelain cup (labeled— dem Hausvater—as every patriarch's own). The soldier portrait thus appears as one contemporary commodity among others, something that was at the time routinely purchased by men in conjunction with their military service, to be displayed in homes from which those men were temporarily or permanently absent [fig. 2.3].

Scholz' peasant family is monstrous—and modern. Outside their window, a late-model threshing machine sits

r m  ,a-m,  '  mnd ho,  aa  e ae  h Beagmrsse n oh ah r h iche Gale e  nse t nt n Arcktiktur

![](images/05e194f31e956a87eb348b7400fa01d847d93282e23bf1798802b37764daceec.jpg)  
Gren.Scifen. aa. 6 Camp Cirofsh-Merltenb. Greu.Rei.Ny N

Pr moro om re mere etra it ofuler n a  o e e leent Nunmr -e  er sr ',  age Fotsg g o ng osen

idle in the field, a montage detail affixed to a glaring yellow painted plane and juxtaposed with a painted preacher approaching with the meal that is on his mind exposed and glowing, comic-book style, beneath his clerical robes, while the Word that he brings issues from his open mouth and his oversized gesturing hand. Inside their living room what may be a newfangled butter-making device sits on a small table, its glass and metal parts painted with machinelike precision, while a sack of feed rests in the corner, displaying a gluedon label and paired with a montage fragment that stands in for a thirty-kilo weight.4 Looking like a monster displaced from a painting by Hieronymus Bosch, the goblin-eared snot-nosed, pimply-skinned boy busies himself with the anal torture of the toad and bears a farm machine patent on his hollow, open head. The father, his head pasted with bank notes, grips his Bible (more automatically than devoutly) and narrows his eyes in an unkind sidewise stare. The mother, a piglet perched on her lap, her head penetrated by a metal screw, just marks time, moronic, wall-eyed, waiting.

Scholz claimed to have based this picture on his own experience as a wounded veteran in 1919, when, seeking food for his hungry family, he asked some well-stocked farmers if they could spare something to eat and was told that the compost heap was at his disposal. Seemingly still seething as he paints and pastes, Scholz takes pains to show in his satire not only the pairing of filthy fingernailed peasant hands and tiny swine hooves and the rhyming of a piglet's snout in the matriarch's lap and a preacher's snout out the window but also the constellation of machines, commodities, patents, and money that make up the business of modern “industrial" farming. Here, hay gets made with the help of the motor-driven thresher while a child's empty head bears the machine's patent. Almost touching the tip of the boy's tensely bent little finger as he torments the toad on the table, the exposed corner of a pasted-on printed page presents, by means of Scholz' mordant montage, the words “Himmelwärts/ Kinderherz," a condensed, ironic caption that pairs the tender terms “heavenward” and “heart of a child” with the sadism of what the demon-child does to the unlucky amphibian. This presentation of typographic fragments in the material form of a cropped page from a contemporary publication demonstrates a use of language that answers the pastor's empty gestures and the patriarch's ready-made drinking cup label with the ironic parataxis of montage. The juxtaposition of pictorial as well as textual montage elements with the monstrousness of painted figures suggests that Scholz intended his viewers to recognize his work as a deliberate debasement not only of painting but also of language. Such debasement, Scholz' work asserts, was the only possible response a picture could offer to the barbarism of contemporary life in post-World War I Germany.s

![](images/d3bf1362932659059aa0ed40a9c105fe416728462183e328e561d8d63c803fb7.jpg)  
Man INofflenbenl. 2. Ee hüing. Hanen Regl

In calling his peasant family “industrial farmers" while depicting them with montage as a supplement to oil painting, Scholz alludes to a contemporary definition of the word montage as a technique for assembling or mounting machine parts in modern industry that the dadaists invoked when they called their process of making pictures montieren. Thus the dadaists, and after them Scholz, associated their production of pictures with the operations of the assembly line and, more broadly, the expanding modernization of Germany's industrial production around 192o, and they did so ambivalently, sometimes praising, sometimes criticizing, but always insisting on the determinative significance of technologies of industrial production and reproduction in contemporary modernity. In his autobiography, written in exile in New York during World War II, Grosz claimed that he had hung an “autographed" portrait of Henry Ford on his studio wall as early as 1916 (of course, Grosz had forged the American industrialist's signature and enthusiastic dedication for himself).16 Whether or not Grosz' fascination with Ford actually began as early as he claimed, certainly when he equated the dadaists' first “photo-gluing-montageexperiments" with “the mounting of machine parts” (as when Höch claimed that the Berlin dadaists' "whole purpose was to integrate objects from the world of machines and industry into the world of art"), the point was to associate Dada montage with modernized industry, in particular the Fordist and Taylorist models that were receiving a great deal of attention in Germany in 1919–20, the years in which the most significant works of Berlin Dada montage were produced.17 Indeed Richard Huelsenbeck's 192o pamphlet, Dada siegt! (Dada Triumphs) [83], makes a point of praising "America, the land of... the Taylor system and the giant slaughterhouses," and the title of one of Grosz' montages "calls for the introduction of the Taylor system in painting.”8

Höch and Hausmann name Hausmann as the initiator of their experiments in photomontage, and both suggest that the impetus for those experiments was a conceptual shift in their understanding of what a picture could be. However, Höch presents Dada photomontage as an “expansion" upon an “idea of working with photographs” that had wide currency in Germany around 1918, while Hausmann asserts that he “invented" photomontage as a medium of Dada art, and that he did so by transforming his nascent practice of collage in the wake of an epiphanic encounter with photomontage soldier portraits. A basic distinction thus emerges between these two accounts of the beginnings of Dada photomontage. For Höch, the dadaists' production of "pictorial photomontage" remained connected to the popular photographic prac tices upon which it expanded, practices that encompassed the anonymous production of lithographic prints and portrait photographs, as well as the clipping and gluing of individual photographic portraits; moreover, the dadaists' own initial experiments themselves represented a more or less collective enterprise, with Höch and Hausmann both beginning to make photomontages upon their return to Berlin. Hausmann's account shows no interest in that aspect of photomontage and what Höch perceived as its potential to alter the conditions of artistic production and authorship; in this Hausmann differs not only from Höch but also from Grosz, Heartfield, and Herzfelde, who at various moments presented their own accounts of the “invention" of Dada photomontage.

Writing in 1928, Grosz told another story of the origins of Dada photomontage. "When John Heartfield and I invented photomontage in my Südende studio at five o'clock one May morning in 1916," he explains:

neither of us had the slightest idea either of the tremendous possibilities or of the thorny but successful path that this discovery would take. As it sometimes is in life, we had without knowing it stumbled upon a gold mine. At that moment young adventurers everywhere were setting out for the unknown land, Dada—certain discoveries are always just hovering in the air, nearly realized. Erwin [Piscator], then still a soldier in Flanders, was one of our own kind. He had already heard of us, and expressed his sympathy by sending packages of tea. (It was the time of artificial honey decorated with the Iron Cross, and that so-called war-marmalade was what one ate on bread— everything obtained with a ration coupon.)9

Unlike Hausmann and Höch, Grosz makes no reference to photography when he speaks of the invention of photomontage. Instead he alludes to the founding of Dada in Zurich in 1916, and he situates the “gold mine" that photomontage would become in relation to Dada as an "unknown land," the tea that the future avant-garde theater director Erwin Piscator sent back from the front in Flanders as care-packages-in-reverse to Grosz' studio in Berlin, and to the patriotically packaged artificial honey that was obtained during the war not with money but with a ration coupon. Thus Grosz accounts for the “invention" of photomontage

by invoking 1916 as a moment in which that medium was “in the air,” awaiting discovery by the future dadaists, who would recognize the connections among care-packages sent from the front, artificial honey, and vouchers. All of which indicates that, for Grosz, the invention of photomontage depended on a recognition of disruptions the war had brought to the economy and its modes of representation, and on an experience of the attendant material conditions.²º

Asked in a 1966 radio interview whether it was true that he had begun as a soldier to send “postcards [pasted with] with pictures from the illustrated newspapers" in order “to circumvent the strict censorship of letters” during World War I, John Heartfield offered an account of the “invention of photomontage” that he insisted “only seemed to contradict” the one that had been published by Grosz in 1928. In a transcript of the interview, Heartfield speaks of “works” that he “made along with other people” as having “showed him the way to the invention of photomontage.” “Those works were postcards," Heartfield explains, “that not only I but also and above all other soldiers often made to get their messages to relatives back home, covertly, so to speak. They were glued postcards."²í As does Höch in her 1966 lecture notes, Heartfield in the radio interview of that same year emphasizes that Dada photomontage emerged in response to a popular practice, in this case once again a practice involving communication between soldiers called up for duty and loved ones left at home.

Where Grosz’ and Heartfield's stories are concerned, there exists another version that in effect combines the two. It was told by the poet and pioneer of avant-garde publishing, Wieland Herzfelde, Heartfield's younger brother, founder of the Malik Verlag, and author of the catalogue to the 192o First International Dada Fair, at which Berlin Dada photomontages were first exhibited. Herzfelde's story of the invention of photomontage was published in 1962 and soon became a key source for the discussion of photomontage in histories of Dada, including Hans Richter's classic Dada: Art and Antí-Art.²² Grosz, Herzfelde writes:

had specialized in sending care-packages to annoy soldiers at the front. As far as he was concerned, they weren't annoyed enough. In late 1916, while I was stationed on the western front for the second time, I received one of his packages. I took great pleasure in the way the field address was written, in the care with which everything was packed, and above all in the irony with which he had selected the gifts. It contained two starched shirt fronts, one white and one with a floral pattern;

a pair of black sleeve protectors; a dainty shoe horn; a set of assorted samples of tea, whose individual handwritten labels claimed that the teas would inspire “Patience,"“Sweet Dreams," "Respect for Authority," and "Loyalty to the Royal Family." Haphazardly glued to a cardboard were advertisements for hernia belts, fraternal song books, and fortified dog food, labels from schnapps and wine bottles, photos from illustrated papers —all clipped at will and assembled absurdly. Soon Grosz and John [Heartfield] began to use for their correspondence similarly hand-finished postcards. Decades later my brother spoke of postcards which were sent back home from the front, and on which photograph clippings had been assembled such that they said in pictures what would have been censored had it been said in words. Some of our friends, among them [Sergei] Tretiakov, created out of that story the legend that the anonymous masses had thus invented photomontage. The truth is that many of those who received the aforementioned cards took pleasure in them and attempted to make something similar. That encouraged Heartfield to develop out of what was originally a politically inflammatory pastime a conscious technique.... Indeed photomontage was more a discovery than an invention.²3

Herzfelde describes a care-package, a container of commodities chosen with irony in mind and agitation as its aim, a repository of, among other things, products that would have been not only useless, but ludicrous at the front in 1916: elements of a dandy's costume (shirt fronts fancy and plain) and of a clerk's (sleeve protectors to absorb wayward ink); a “dainty shoe horn" unlikely to do much good on combat boots; customized tea bags with labels invoking the values, habits, and slogans of the Wilhelmine empire; and a cardboard decorated with advertisements and pictures taken from newspapers. Juxtaposed with labels from schnapps and wine bottles and photographs from the illustrated papers (items that had a place both in Grosz' studio and in the trenches), the collection of advertisements for a belt to support a strained groin muscle, for songs to stir the patriotic soul, and for specialty dog food, introduces a particularly bitter form of irony that would flourish in Berlin Dada. Placed alongside items that at the time were regularly sent from Berlin and other German cities to provide comfort and pleasure to soldiers at the front, a product manufactured, packaged, and advertised for canine consumption becomes an emblem of barbarism. Late 1916, the montage in Herzfelde's recollection suggests, was no time to be promoting fortified food for dogs; that is, not if the product was really meant for canine consumption, and not if the advertisement was

anything but calculated cynicism. Human beings, after all, were hungry in large numbers.

The inclusion of the dog-food advertisement in the care-package montage establishes an ethical dimension of Berlin Dada, in this case by means of the juxtaposition of the callous promotion of commodities in a time of war and deprivation with the care understood to inhere in the assembling of packages that in German are called Liebesgaben (gifts of love). As a supplement to the organized production of carepackages, groups of women in wartime Germany gathered in so-called Klebestuben (glue-rooms) to assemble albums composed of newspaper and magazine clippings, postcards, and reproductions of works of art [fig. 2.4].²4 The carepackages and the montages that Grosz is said to have sent to his friends at the front therefore demand to be situated in relation to a widespread popular practice of making montages on the pages of what were known as Klebebücher (gluebooks), a term that anticipates the dadaists' description of their own first pictorial montages as Klebebilder (glue-pictures).

Apart from the dog food, the other items whose advertisement Herzfelde mentions—hernia belts and fraternal song books—could often be seen among the commodities promoted in wartime advertising. Song books were marketed as gifts to lift morale and to encourage sing-along camaraderie among soldiers at the front, and hernia belts are not hard to find in the back pages of the wartime illustrated press. Grosz' mention of ersatz honey as a sign of the times in Germany during World War I, and as a touchstone for the invention of photomontage, may on first reading have seemed fanciful. But the marketing of artificial honey in wartime Germany can tell us more about Dada photomontage and the culture in which it emerged than we might think. Even if the Iron Cross as trademark was a dadaist flourish in Grosz' anecdote, photomontage as purportedly invented by Grosz and Heartfield was at least partly a response to the marketplace in which a “Hurrah!" and a Heil und Sieg were used to promote a honey substitute. In Herzfelde's story, cardboard montage enclosures quickly led to montage postcards independently mailed to the front. Then the soldiers who received those postcards produced their own in response, sending them back along the same postal route. But Herzfelde cautions that even if soldiers came to fashion montage postcards in the trenches, photomontage cannot exactly be seen as an invention of the anonymous masses. Nonetheless, as Herzfelde tells it, the soldiers' response did encourage Heartfield to develop out of a “politically inflammatory pastime” a “conscious technique," which is to say a pictorial medium characterized by certain consistent practices and ambitions. Although Heartfield's typographic designs for Neue Jugend [84] and other Malik Verlag publications in 1917 and 1918 demonstrate a range of approaches to the selection and arrangement of materials that were crucial to Berlin Dada montage, no photomontage by Heartfield or Grosz can be dated securely before 1919.

Mab   (-    1. Coun     b urg

![](images/4b9e457d52dc9f2f651589c351e25662dfc75b147e8d286664a5a445d6982600.jpg)

In 193o Johannes Baader staked his own claim to have made the first Dada montages in the form of “letters” he sent to Raoul Hausmann's wife, Elfriede Hausmann-Schaeffer. Those letters, he explains, were “communications" that meant to exploit the exceptional quantity and variety of “possibilities and stimuli for association” provided by montage technique" in order to convey, more perspicuously than a newspaper, “extremely rich content" in a “condensed, visually striking form."²5 Although Hausmann confirmed Baader's story about the letters and dated them to March 1918, the first example of such work by Baader that has survived is a photomontage postcard given to Hannah Höch as a birthday greeting on 1 November 1919 [fig. 2.5].²6 Addressed, in fragments of cut-and-pasted typography, to “H—ann—a dada,” the postcard includes miniature portrait photographs of Baader and Hausmann, each labeled with the name of the other. The portrait of Hausmann, who may have collaborated with Baader in producing the montage, takes the place of a postage stamp, while the portrait of Baader appears on the other side of the postcard, which ordinarily would display the missive's picture of a landscape, monument, work of art, or some other typical postcard subject. Along the bottom of the Baader-portrait side of the card, a strip of pasted paper shows the incomplete phrase “Legen Sie Ihr Geld in da” with the “a” cut in half as the paper wraps around the right-hand edge of the card, splitting the word “dada” in two, and compelling the postcard's beholder to turn the card around in her hand in order to read the rest of the word and the injunction of which it is part: “Legen Sie Ihr Geld in dada an!" (Invest your money in dada!). Another narrow band of paper wraps around the center of the postcard to cover the “h” in “Ihr," as if to suggest that the work of montage should be seen as penetrating the “you” to whom it is addressed, just as that

2.5 Job   e r  e Baber rt '  sod o o o od 'n ba n  ant an us ns. Phochi u Aihtur  
![](images/58ff26c969a1d1abcb76ce2352c240be0021193f0ec40561c9f51f98a5c76eb1.jpg)
